patches.find: The closing tell is the ratio itself: v_a/c = 1.574 = 20,015 / 12,717 = r_disc / disc_thickness. The dome's two 'different' derivations are the same derivation; v_a was chosen precisely so the live-page numbers reproduce the wins.html numbers.
patches.replace: The closing tell is the ratio itself: v_a/c = 1.574 = 20,015 / 12,717 = r_disc / disc_thickness. The 0.2% offset between v_a/c = 1.574 and the textbook πc/2-over-c value 1.5708 is exactly the offset between the dome's disc_thickness = 12,717 km and Earth's diameter 2 × R<sub>Earth</sub> = 12,742 km — v_a was fit to absorb that residual, so f = v_a / (2 × r_disc) and f = c / (2 × disc_thickness) agree at 11.787 Hz by construction, not by independent physics. The dome's two 'different' derivations are the same derivation; v_a was chosen precisely so the live-page numbers reproduce the wins.html numbers.
patches.rationale: ISS-1315: makes the 0.2% offset explicit so the by-construction agreement is visible in situ.

patches.find:
This is the diagnostic signature of per-WIN parameter fitting, not unified physical modeling.

Tesla's location destroys the dome interpretation.
patches.replace:
This is the diagnostic signature of per-WIN parameter fitting, not unified physical modeling. A dispersion-law defense — 'different modes on a dispersive medium have different phase velocities, so c for WIN-029 and v_a for WIN-001 can both be right on the same circuit' — fails on its own terms: the dome publishes no dispersion relation ω(k), no mode index, and no cavity eigenvalue calculation that would justify the per-WIN speed selection. The c-vs-v_a choice is operational (whatever number reproduces the published Hz value), not theoretical (a mode formula's output).

Tesla's location destroys the dome interpretation.
patches.rationale: ISS-1316: preempts the dispersive-medium defender retreat in situ.

patches.find: WIN-029 is the credit claim for that exact inversion. A retreat to transverse modes does not save WIN-038 in isolation
patches.replace: WIN-029 is the credit claim for that exact inversion. The dome's wins.html scorecard records this in its own words: 'WIN-029 — Schumann Resonance Requires Hard Conductive Ceiling CONFIRMED (H = c/(4×7.83) = 9,572 km, 5.3% from model).' The quarter-wave formula is framed by the dome itself as the equation that produces the confirmation — not as a simplified heuristic to be set aside. A retreat to transverse modes does not save WIN-038 in isolation
patches.rationale: ISS-1340: inserts verbatim WIN-029 scorecard quote to preempt the quarter-wave-is-just-a-heuristic retreat.

patches.find: The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: a broad family of smooth monotonic H(r) functions fitted to the same sun/moon altitude calibration targets, combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention, produces a latitude-dependent ratio of effectively the same form over the relevant r range, so matching one parametrization to 0.1% does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function.
patches.replace: The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: alternative smooth monotonically-decreasing H(r) functions calibrated to the same two anchors (sun altitude at r_sun and moon altitude at r_moon), combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention, should produce closely-similar FSF curves over the dome's 0–14,000 km station-radius range, because H(r)/r over a finite range is constrained primarily by the calibration anchors rather than the specific interpolating family. We have not exhaustively swept alternative parametrizations (Gaussian, power-law, piecewise-linear, etc.) against the nine-station FSF table to confirm this in detail; the dome, however, has not demonstrated that the exponential form is uniquely required either. Matching one parametrization to 0.1% therefore does not discriminate the dome from any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function — but the strength of the non-discrimination claim is bounded by the (still open) exhaustivity check.
patches.rationale: ISS-1438: softens unsupported universal quantifier and adds explicit exhaustivity caveat.

patches.find: The dome's claimed Bermuda/Japan symmetry has no factual basis.
patches.replace: The dome's claimed Bermuda/Japan symmetry lacks any published mechanism; both zones sit at similar latitudes (~25°N), not in opposite hemispheres, and the antipodal tolerance invoked to claim the match would implicate roughly a third of the globe.
patches.rationale: ISS-1978 (WIN-028.c7 H0): TLDR overreach fix — detail concedes real geographic observation, TLDR must not overstate.

patches.find: NOAA and Lloyd's confirm no anomalous loss rates in either region
patches.replace: No anomalous loss rates (NOAA/Lloyd's); claimed antipodal symmetry fails geographic precision test
patches.rationale: ISS-1979 (WIN-028.c7 H1): finding update to also capture the precision-test argument.

patches.find: The two folklore zones do sit at roughly opposite hemispheres in this loose sense, and that is a real geographic observation.
patches.replace: The two folklore zones sit at roughly antipodal longitudes, and that is a real geographic observation. (They are not, however, in opposite hemispheres: both Bermuda at ~25°N and the Devil's Sea at ~25–35°N are in the northern hemisphere.)
patches.rationale: ISS-1980 (WIN-028.c7 H2): fix imprecise 'opposite hemispheres' — both zones are northern hemisphere.

patches.find: The mechanism (steelman test). The dome publishes no mechanism for the claimed symmetry. We test the only standard physical mechanism
patches.replace: The mechanism (our steelman test). The dome publishes no mechanism for the claimed symmetry. We test the only standard physical mechanism
patches.rationale: ISS-1981 (WIN-028.c7 H3): clarify heading — add 'our' to make clear this is our charitable steelman, not the dome's claim.

patches.find: fits the pattern Lakatos (1970, Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes) identified as a degenerative research programme — auxiliary conditions tightened to protect the programme from observations that have gone wrong, without generating new predictive content;
patches.replace: fits the pattern Lakatos (1970, Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes) identified as a degenerative research programme — auxiliary conditions tightened to protect the programme from observations that have gone wrong, without generating new predictive content. The mapping to Lakatos's canonical pattern (where defenders add auxiliary hypotheses, as in the Prout–Bohr case) is direct: a falsification rule narrowed after observation to exclude exactly the observations that fell foul of the old rule is functionally equivalent to introducing a new auxiliary hypothesis — 'rows of this kind do not falsify the model' — solely to protect the programme. The structural move is the same; only the surface form (rule-tightening vs hypothesis-adding) differs;
patches.rationale: ISS-1407: adds one-sentence Lakatos gloss mapping rule-tightening to auxiliary-hypothesis addition, closing the rhetorical gap curmudgeon identified.
